title: Pashinyan on Russian peacekeepers
content:
Pashinyan stated that Armenia is a firm supporter of the presence of Russian peacekeeping troops in Nagorno-Karabakh, but it is unacceptable for them to become "a silent witness to the depopulation of the Nagorno-Karabakh region." He emphasized that the closure of the Lachin Corridor is a gross violation of point 6 of the tripartite statement of November 9, 2020.

2. **[Almaty Declaration and the status of Artsakh](https://infocom.am/en/article/93329)** – Eduard Aghajanyan, Chairman of the Standing Committee on Foreign Relations, addressed Lavrov's statement regarding the Almaty Declaration and the question of the status of Nagorno-Karabakh. He stated that by referring to the Almaty declaration, no opportunity was lost, and it does not mean that there will not be an opportunity to address the status in the future.

3. **[Daily News Digest – October 29, 2021](https://infocom.am/en/Article/67228)** – PACE co-rapporteurs planned a fact-finding visit to Armenia in November 2021, with discussions focusing on electoral reform, judicial independence, the media situation, and the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict and its consequences on democratic institutions in Armenia.

4. **[Alen Simonyan on Azerbaijan and Lachin Corridor](https://infocom.am/en/article/93431)** – Speaker Alen Simonyan stated that Armenia's task is to protect the population of Nagorno-Karabakh, noting that Azerbaijan had started announcing that the Nagorno-Karabakh problem no longer exists and demanded Russian peacekeepers stop using the name "Nagorno-Karabakh" and Armenian place names. He also warned of Azerbaijan's intention to close the Lachin Corridor, calling it "the only full-fledged road of humanitarian significance for the livelihood of the Armenians of Nagorno-Karabakh."

5. **[Armenia-Russia relations discussion](https://infocom.am/en/article/84439)** – A sharp decline in Russian soft power polling in Armenia was attributed to the change in security environment and the defeat in the Second Karabakh War. Both Armenia and Azerbaijan viewed Moscow as the most important mediator or decision-maker in the Karabakh conflict.

These articles span from 2021 to 2024 and cover various aspects of the Nagorno-Karabakh situation as reported by Infocom.am.

title: Armenia's Corruption Perception Index decreased by one point: Transparency International
content: Armenia's Corruption Perception Index (CPI) score for 2025 decreased by one point compared to the previous year, dropping to 46 on a scale of 0 (highly corrupt) to 100 (very clean). Transparency International published the 2025 CPI results showing corruption is deepening worldwide. Armenia's score is the average of 6 sources including the Bertelsmann Foundation's 2026 Transformation Index (51 points) and the Freedom House Nations in Transit 2024 report's Corruption sub-index (44 points). In the CPI ranking of 182 countries, Armenia shares 65th-69th places with Kuwait, Montenegro, Namibia, and Senegal (in 2024, Armenia shared 63rd-64th places among 180 countries). Among Council of Europe member states, Armenia shares 30th-31st places with Montenegro, and only three EU member countries (Romania, Bulgaria, and Hungary) have CPI scores lower than Armenia's. Armenia's CPI score (46) is higher than the global average which fell from 43 to 42 — the lowest in the last decade. Among neighbors: Turkey scored 31 (124th-129th places), Iran 23 (153rd-156th places), Azerbaijan 30 (130th-134th places). Among EAEU countries: Kazakhstan 38, Belarus 31, Kyrgyzstan 26, Russia 22.

title: Suspect Donations and Unidentified Sources of the Ruling Civil Contract's Election Campaign Finances
content: An investigation found that transactions associated with the election campaign of the ruling Civil Contract party are suspicious, with some finances having undisclosed origins. A woman named Karine unwillingly became one of the donors of the Civil Contract's election campaign on the eve of the 2023 Yerevan Council of Elders elections. Her ID card and signature were exploited to channel funds of unverified origin into the Civil Contract party's account. The legal maximum donation allowed per person is 2.5 million AMD (approximately 6,200 USD). A significant 87% of contributions to the Civil Contract party's election campaign were amassed through transfers exceeding 1 million AMD. Within this category, around 70% of donations were made by 140 individuals, each contributing the maximum allowable amount of 2.5 million AMD. Three suspicious patterns were identified: individuals contributing donations ranging from 1 to 2.5 million AMD reside in addresses very close to each other; individuals affiliated with large business companies made substantial donations; and prominent contributors include officials, municipal council candidates and current members, and people affiliated with them. Syuzanna Sayadyan, a member of the Council of Elders of the Artashat community, contributed 2.5 million AMD, despite her declared income for 2022 amounting to only 702,975 AMD — received from the Civil Contract party as a salary. One resident of Nor Nork who served as a proxy for the Civil Contract party stated he had transferred 1 million AMD, but the list indicated 2.5 million AMD under his name. The findings conclude that an organized mechanism was employed to channel funds into financing the election campaign of the Civil Contract party, involving associating sums with names of individuals who were often unaware of the process.

title: Stefano Tomat presented to Armen Grigoryan the works to be carried out by the EU mission in Armenia
content: On February 21, Security Council chief of staff Armen Grigoryan received the delegation headed by Stefano Tomat, commander of civil operations of the EU External Action Service. Welcoming the arrival and official launch of the new EU civilian monitoring mission in Armenia, the Security Council Secretary expressed confidence that it will continue to contribute to the establishment of stability and peace in the region. Tomat thanked the Armenian side for their support of the EU civil mission and presented the works to be carried out in Armenia. On January 23, the EU Council reached an agreement on creating a European Union civilian mission in Armenia. The mission started its work on February 20. EEAS Managing Director Stefano Tomat will serve as the Civilian Operation Commander, while Markus Ritter will serve as the Head of Mission. Through its deployment on the Armenian side of the Armenia-Azerbaijan border, the objectives are to contribute to stability in the border areas of Armenia, build confidence and human security in conflict-affected areas, and ensure an environment conducive to the normalization efforts between Armenia and Azerbaijan supported by the EU. The total exclusively civilian staff of the EUMA will be approximately 100, including around 50 unarmed observers. The Operational Headquarters of the mission will be in Yeghegnadzor, in Armenia's Vayots Dzor province.
